schools.summary: Hannah's vow, that if God would give her a son she would give him back to the LORD all his days: 'and she vowed a vow, and said, O LORD of hosts, if thou wilt indeed look on the affliction of thine handmaid, and remember me, and not forget thine handmaid, but wilt give unto thine handmaid a man child, then I will give him unto the LORD all the days of his life, and there shall no razor come upon his head' — the consecrated petition — 'and she vowed a vow... if thou wilt indeed look on the affliction of thine handmaid... but wilt give unto thine handmaid a man child, then I will give him unto the LORD all the days of his life, and there shall no razor come upon his head': Hannah asks a son not for herself but to devote him to God as a Nazarite (the witnesses noting the significance — the rare and beautiful character of Hannah's prayer, that she sought the very gift she most desired not to keep for her own comfort but to give back wholly to the LORD, vowing that the son she begged should be a Nazarite from the womb, no razor on his head, and dedicated to God's service all his life, so that her petition was at once the cry of a longing heart and the surrender of the gift before it was given, faith asking only that it might consecrate, the affliction spread before God, the son sought to be God's, the gift devoted in the very asking, the lesson — that the prayer most acceptable to God asks his gifts not to consume them on self but to use them for his glory, 'ye ask, and receive not, because ye ask amiss, that ye may consume it upon your lusts,' James 4:3, the truth that the believer's best desires are those that would turn the answer into an offering, devoting to God what they receive from him, 'of thine own have we given thee,' 1 Chronicles 29:14, the figure — the faith that asks in order to consecrate, fulfilled in every soul that seeks God's blessings to lay them again at his feet, 'I beseech you... that ye present your bodies a living sacrifice,' Romans 12:1, the truth that what is sought for God and vowed to him is given and blessed, the figure of the prayer of faith that asks God's gift only to devote it wholly to him, the call to seek God's gifts for his glory and not our own, the assurance that God delights to answer the prayer that would give back to him); Hannah spreading her affliction before the LORD of hosts and begging him to remember her with a son, the cry of the longing heart (the affliction before God, James 4:3) — the petition (give thine handmaid a man child); 'then I will give him unto the LORD all the days of his life, and there shall no razor come upon his head' (Hannah vowing to give the son back to the LORD all his life as a Nazarite, the gift consecrated in the very asking, faith asking only that it might devote, of God's own giving him back to God, the consecrated gift, 1 Chronicles 29:14; Romans 12:1) — the figure (the faith that asks in order to consecrate, the providence, the vow, Eli observing her silent prayer to follow, vv.12-13) — Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: What does Hannah's vow to give her son to the LORD reveal about her prayer?

sides.position: The prayer of faith that asks God's gift only to devote it wholly to him - Hannah seeking the gift she most desired not to keep but to give back wholly to the LORD, vowing the son a Nazarite dedicated all his life, the gift consecrated in the very asking, the prayer that asks God's gifts for his glory not self (James 4:3), the believer devoting to God what he receives (1 Chronicles 29:14), the faith that asks in order to consecrate (Romans 12:1)

schools.summary: That Hannah prayed in her heart, her lips moving but no voice heard: 'now Hannah, she spake in her heart; only her lips moved, but her voice was not heard: therefore Eli thought she had been drunken' — the prayer of the heart — 'now Hannah, she spake in her heart; only her lips moved, but her voice was not heard: therefore Eli thought she had been drunken': Hannah's prayer was an inward, heartfelt pouring out of the soul, audible to God alone (the witnesses noting the significance — the true nature of acceptable prayer, that it is the speaking of the heart and not merely of the lips, Hannah pouring out her soul to God in words formed within but not uttered aloud, so that no ear but God's could hear, the prayer accepted not for its sound but for its sincerity, the heart's desire that God reads though no voice reaches man, the lips moving with the heart's earnestness, yet the voice unheard, the prayer that needs no words to reach God, Eli's mistaking the inward fervour for drunkenness, the world not understanding the prayer of the heart, the lesson — that God hears the prayer of the heart, and that true prayer is the desire of the soul rather than the noise of the lips, 'the LORD looketh on the heart,' 1 Samuel 16:7, the truth that the inward groaning the Spirit forms in the heart is heard of God though it find no words, 'the Spirit itself maketh intercession for us with groanings which cannot be uttered,' Romans 8:26, the figure — the secret prayer that the Father who seeth in secret hears and rewards, fulfilled in him who bids us enter our closet and shut the door, 'pray to thy Father which is in secret; and thy Father which seeth in secret shall reward thee openly,' Matthew 6:6, the truth that the worth of prayer is in the heart and not the voice, and God hears what man cannot, the figure of the inward and heartfelt prayer that needs no words, the prayer God hears though man does not, the call to pray from the heart and not for the ear of men, the assurance that God hears the silent cry of the soul); Hannah's prayer being the speaking of the heart, her lips moving with the heart's earnestness yet no voice heard, audible to God alone (the prayer of the heart, 1 Samuel 16:7) — the prayer (she spake in her heart); 'therefore Eli thought she had been drunken' (Eli mistaking Hannah's inward fervour for drunkenness, the world not understanding the prayer of the heart, the groaning of the soul that God hears though it find no words, the secret prayer the Father hears, the inward prayer God reads, Romans 8:26; debates.question: What does Hannah's silent prayer reveal about true prayer?

sides.position: The inward and heartfelt prayer that needs no words, the prayer God hears though man does not - the speaking of the heart and not merely the lips, accepted for its sincerity not its sound, God reading the heart's desire though no voice reaches man (1 Samuel 16:7), the Spirit's groanings that cannot be uttered yet heard of God (Romans 8:26), the secret prayer the Father who seeth in secret hears (Matthew 6:6)

schools.summary: That Hannah, having prayed, went her way and ate, her countenance no more sad: 'and she said, Let thine handmaid find grace in thy sight. So the woman went her way, and did eat, and her countenance was no more sad' — the peace that follows believing prayer — 'and she said, Let thine handmaid find grace in thy sight. So the woman went her way, and did eat, and her countenance was no more sad': Hannah, having cast her burden on the LORD and received Eli's blessing, is comforted and at rest before any answer is seen (the witnesses noting the significance — the blessed change in Hannah, who, having poured out her soul to God and committed her cause to him, now rises with a lightened heart, eats again where before she could not, and wears no more her sad countenance, not because her petition was yet granted but because she had left it with God in faith and rested in his will, the very mark of true prayer that it brings peace and quiet to the soul before the answer comes, the burden rolled on God, the heart eased by committing the cause, the peace that springs from faith and not from sight, the lesson — that the believer who truly commits his way to God in prayer finds rest and peace, the anxious care exchanged for quietness of heart, 'be careful for nothing; but in every thing by prayer... and the peace of God... shall keep your hearts,' Philippians 4:6-7, the truth that faith, having cast its burden on the LORD, is sustained and stayed, 'cast thy burden upon the LORD, and he shall sustain thee,' Psalm 55:22, the figure — the soul that, having prayed in faith, goes its way in peace, leaving the matter with God, fulfilled in the rest of those who roll their cares on him, 'casting all your care upon him; for he careth for you,' 1 Peter 5:7, the truth that the peace of the believer rests not on the answer received but on the God to whom the prayer is committed, the figure of the peace of faith that rests in God after committing its burden to him in prayer, the call to leave our burdens with God and rise in peace, the assurance that faith finds rest in God before it sees the answer); Hannah humbly asking to find grace, receiving Eli's word as a token of favour, the believing heart taking comfort (the request for grace, Philippians 4:6-7) — the request (find grace in thy sight); 'So the woman went her way, and did eat, and her countenance was no more sad' (Hannah going her way comforted, eating again and her sadness gone, not because the answer was seen but because she had left her cause with God in faith and rested in his will, the burden cast on the LORD, the peace that follows believing prayer, Psalm 55:22; 1 Peter 5:7) — the figure (the soul that, having prayed in faith, goes its way in peace, the providence, the peace, the family returning home and Hannah conceiving to follow, vv.19-20) — Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: What does Hannah's countenance being no more sad reveal?

sides.position: The peace of faith that rests in God after committing its burden to him in prayer - Hannah comforted before any answer is seen because she left her cause with God in faith, the mark of true prayer that it brings peace before the answer, the believer who commits his way finding the peace of God (Philippians 4:6-7), faith casting its burden on the LORD and being sustained (Psalm 55:22; 1 Peter 5:7)

themes: And she said, Let thine handmaid find grace in thy sight. So the woman went her way, and did eat, and her countenance was no more sad.
That Hannah, having prayed, went her way and ate, her countenance no more sad (the crux of the peace of faith that rests in God after committing its burden to him in prayer)

the blessed change in Hannah, who, having poured out her soul and committed her cause to God, rises with a lightened heart and wears no more her sad countenance, not because her petition was granted but because she left it with God in faith

the mark of true prayer that it brings peace before the answer comes, the believer who commits his way finding the peace of God keeping his heart, faith casting its burden on the LORD and being sustained (Philippians 4:6-7; Psalm 55:22; 1 Peter 5:7)

schools.summary: The family worshipping and returning home, and the LORD remembering Hannah: 'and they rose up in the morning early, and worshipped before the LORD, and returned, and came to their house to Ramah: and Elkanah knew Hannah his wife; and the LORD remembered her' — the family worshipped and returned, and God remembered Hannah's prayer (the and they rose up in the morning early, and worshipped before the LORD, and returned, and came to their house to Ramah, the worship and return, the and Elkanah knew Hannah his wife; and the LORD remembered her, the LORD remembered, the figure, the God who remembers the prayer of the afflicted and answers in his time, the truth that the LORD remembered Hannah and would grant her petition, Genesis 30:22, the figure of the God who remembers and answers the believing prayer, the worship and return, the LORD remembered, vv.11); the family worshipping and returning home (the devotion, Genesis 30:22) — the worship (and returned to Ramah); 'the LORD remembered her' (God remembering Hannah's prayer, the answer, the figure, the God who remembers and answers the believing prayer, the providence, the remembering, Hannah conceiving and bearing Samuel to follow, vv.20) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: The birth of Samuel, whom Hannah named because she had asked him of the LORD: 'wherefore it came to pass, when the time was come about after Hannah had conceived, that she bare a son, and called his name Samuel, saying, Because I have asked him of the LORD' — the child of prayer — 'she bare a son, and called his name Samuel, saying, Because I have asked him of the LORD': Hannah names her son to mark him forever as the gift of God in answer to prayer (the witnesses noting the significance — the fitness and beauty of the name Samuel, asked of God, or heard of God, by which Hannah set upon her son a perpetual memorial of the prayer that begged him and the God who granted him, so that the child's very name should declare to all generations that he was the fruit of believing prayer and the gift of a faithful God, the answer wearing the mark of the asking, the name a monument of the prayer heard, the child confessed to be God's gift, the memorial of grace borne in the name, the lesson — that the good things we receive from God are to be owned as the answers to prayer and held as his gifts, named and remembered as such, 'I love the LORD, because he hath heard my voice and my supplications,' Psalm 116:1, the truth that what is asked of God in faith and received is to be marked and confessed as his gift, the answer traced to the asking, 'ask, and ye shall receive, that your joy may be full,' John 16:24, the figure — the child of prayer that bears the name of the answer, fulfilled in every blessing the believer receives as the fruit of supplication and owns as God's gift, 'this is the confidence that we have in him... we know that we have the petitions that we desired of him,' 1 John 5:14-15, the truth that the gifts of God are sweeter when received as answers to prayer and named to his praise, the figure of the child of prayer, the gift that bears the name of the asking and the answer, the call to own our mercies as answers to prayer, the assurance that God hears and gives, and his gifts may be named for his grace); Hannah bearing the son in answer to her prayer, the gift God had granted in his time (the answer to prayer, Psalm 116:1) — the birth (she bare a son); 'and called his name Samuel, saying, Because I have asked him of the LORD' (Hannah naming the child Samuel, asked of God, a perpetual memorial that he was the fruit of believing prayer and the gift of a faithful God, the answer wearing the mark of the asking, the gift named for the prayer, John 16:24; 1 John 5:14-15) — the figure (the child of prayer that bears the name of the answer, the providence, the naming, Hannah keeping the child till weaned to follow, vv.21-23) — Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: What does the name Samuel, 'asked of the LORD,' signify?

sides.position: The child of prayer, the gift that bears the name of the asking and the answer - Hannah setting on her son a perpetual memorial of the prayer that begged him and the God who granted him, the answer wearing the mark of the asking, the good things received from God owned as answers to prayer (Psalm 116:1), what is asked in faith marked as his gift (John 16:24), the confidence that God hears and gives (1 John 5:14-15)

themes: Wherefore it came to pass, when the time was come about after Hannah had conceived, that she bare a son, and called his name Samuel, saying, Because I have asked him of the LORD.

the fitness of the name 'asked of God,' by which Hannah set upon her son a perpetual memorial of the prayer that begged him and the God who granted him, the answer wearing the mark of the asking

the good things received from God owned as answers to prayer and his gifts, what is asked in faith and received marked and confessed as his gift, the confidence that God hears and gives (Psalm 116:1; John 16:24; 1 John 5:14-15)

schools.summary: Elkanah going up to offer the yearly sacrifice and his vow: 'and the man Elkanah, and all his house, went up to offer unto the LORD the yearly sacrifice, and his vow' — Elkanah went up with his house to pay the yearly sacrifice and his vow (the and the man Elkanah, and all his house, went up to offer unto the LORD the yearly sacrifice, the yearly sacrifice, the and his vow, his vow paid, the figure, the faithful household keeping its worship and paying its vows, the truth that Elkanah went up to pay the yearly sacrifice and his vow, Deuteronomy 23:21, the figure of the believer who keeps his worship and pays his vows to God, the worship of Elkanah, the vow paid, vv.3); Elkanah going up with the yearly sacrifice (the worship, Deuteronomy 23:21) — the sacrifice (yearly); 'and his vow' (the vow connected with the child to be paid, the discharge, the figure, the believer who keeps his worship and pays his vows to God, the providence, the worship, Hannah staying with the child to follow, vv.22) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: Hannah staying till the child was weaned, then to bring him to abide before the LORD: 'but Hannah went not up; for she said unto her husband, I will not go up until the child be weaned, and then I will bring him, that he may appear before the LORD, and there abide for ever' — Hannah purposed to keep the child till weaned and then dedicate him to the LORD for life (the but Hannah went not up; for she said unto her husband, I will not go up until the child be weaned, the staying, the and then I will bring him, that he may appear before the LORD, and there abide for ever, the dedication purposed, the figure, the mother keeping the child for a season, then giving him wholly to God, the truth that Hannah purposed to dedicate Samuel to the LORD for life, Ruth 1:17, the figure of the gift kept for a time and then wholly devoted, the purpose of Hannah, the child to abide before the LORD, vv.11); Hannah staying till the child was weaned (the purpose, Ruth 1:17) — the staying (until weaned); 'I will bring him, that he may appear before the LORD, and there abide for ever' (Samuel to be dedicated to the LORD for life, the devotion, the figure, the gift kept for a time and then wholly devoted, the providence, the purpose, Elkanah agreeing and Hannah nursing the child to follow, vv.23) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: Elkanah agreeing, and Hannah nursing the child till weaned: 'and Elkanah her husband said unto her, Do what seemeth thee good; tarry until thou have weaned him; only the LORD establish his word. schools.summary: Hannah bringing the weaned child with offerings to the house of the LORD: 'and when she had weaned him, she took him up with her, with three bullocks, and one ephah of flour, and a bottle of wine, and brought him unto the house of the LORD in Shiloh: and the child was young' — Hannah brought the weaned child with generous offerings to dedicate him (the and when she had weaned him, she took him up with her, with three bullocks, and one ephah of flour, and a bottle of wine, the offerings, the and brought him unto the house of the LORD in Shiloh: and the child was young, the child brought, the figure, the mother fulfilling her vow, bringing the child with thank offerings, the truth that Hannah brought Samuel with offerings to the LORD's house, Deuteronomy 16:16, the figure of the believer who pays the vow with thankful offerings, the bringing of Samuel, the offerings of Hannah, vv.11); Hannah bringing generous offerings (the thank offering, Deuteronomy 16:16) — the offerings (three bullocks, flour, wine); 'brought him unto the house of the LORD in Shiloh' (Samuel brought to be dedicated, the vow paid, the figure, the believer who pays the vow with thankful offerings, the providence, the bringing, the presenting of the child to Eli to follow, vv.25-28) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: The bullock slain and the child brought to Eli: 'and they slew a bullock, and brought the child to Eli' — the sacrifice was offered and Samuel presented to Eli (the and they slew a bullock, the bullock slain, the and brought the child to Eli, the child brought to Eli, the figure, the sacrifice offered and the child presented, the dedication begun, the truth that they offered the bullock and brought Samuel to the priest, Leviticus 1:5, the figure of the dedication made with sacrifice, the bullock slain, the child presented, vv.24); the bullock slain in offering (the sacrifice, Leviticus 1:5) — the sacrifice (slew a bullock); 'brought the child to Eli' (Samuel presented to the priest, the dedication, the figure, the dedication made with sacrifice, the providence, the presenting, Hannah's words to Eli to follow, vv.26-28) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: Hannah reminding Eli she was the woman who had prayed there: 'and she said, Oh my lord, as thy soul liveth, my lord, I am the woman that stood by thee here, praying unto the LORD' — Hannah recalled to Eli the prayer he had witnessed (the and she said, Oh my lord, as thy soul liveth, my lord, the address, the I am the woman that stood by thee here, praying unto the LORD, the recollection, the figure, the believer recalling the prayer that God had now answered, the testimony of answered prayer, the truth that Hannah reminded Eli of the prayer he had seen, 1 Samuel 1:13, the figure of the soul that owns and recalls its answered prayer, the address to Eli, the prayer recalled, vv.13); Hannah's earnest address to Eli (the appeal, 1 Samuel 1:13) — the address (oh my lord); 'I am the woman that stood by thee here, praying unto the LORD' (Hannah recalling the prayer, the testimony, the figure, the soul that owns and recalls its answered prayer, the providence, the recollection, Hannah declaring the child the answer to follow, vv.27) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: Hannah declaring the child the answer to her prayer: 'for this child I prayed; and the LORD hath given me my petition which I asked of him' — Hannah owned the child as the direct answer to her prayer (the for this child I prayed, the prayer, the and the LORD hath given me my petition which I asked of him, the answer given, the figure, the believer owning the gift as the answer to prayer, the testimony of God's faithfulness, the truth that Hannah owned Samuel as the answer to her petition, Psalm 116:1, the figure of the soul that traces its mercy to its prayer and to God, the testimony of Hannah, the petition granted, vv.20); Hannah owning that she had prayed for the child (the testimony, Psalm 116:1) — the prayer (for this child I prayed); 'the LORD hath given me my petition which I asked of him' (the child the answer to her petition, the grant, the figure, the soul that traces its mercy to its prayer and to God, the providence, the testimony, Hannah lending the child to the LORD to follow, vv.28) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: Hannah lending Samuel to the LORD as long as he lived: 'therefore also I have lent him to the LORD; as long as he liveth he shall be lent to the LORD. And he worshipped the LORD there' — the gift given back to God — 'therefore also I have lent him to the LORD; as long as he liveth he shall be lent to the LORD. And he worshipped the LORD there': Hannah devotes Samuel wholly to God, giving back the gift she had received in answer to prayer (the witnesses noting the significance — the crown and completion of Hannah's faith, that having received the son she begged, she now surrenders him entirely to the LORD's service for life, the word 'lent', better, 'granted' or 'returned', expressing that she gives back to God what God had given her, holding the gift not for herself but for him, so that the child of prayer becomes the child of dedication, devoted to the sanctuary all his days, the gift received and the gift returned, the surrender of the dearest to God, the dedication for life, not for a season, the lesson — that what we receive from God we are to hold as his and devote to him, giving back our best and dearest to his service, 'of thine own have we given thee,' 1 Chronicles 29:14, the truth that the believer's children, gifts, and self are to be wholly consecrated to the LORD, 'I beseech you... that ye present your bodies a living sacrifice, holy, acceptable unto God,' Romans 12:1, the figure — the soul that surrenders its dearest treasure to God, giving back the gift for his glory, fulfilled in the dedication of all we are and have to him who gave us all, 'ye are not your own... therefore glorify God,' 1 Corinthians 6:19-20, the truth that the highest use of God's gifts is to return them to him in devotion, the figure of the believer who gives back to God the very gift received from him, devoting it wholly to his service, the call to consecrate to God what we have received of him, the assurance that what is given back to God is best kept and most blessed); Hannah giving back to the LORD the very son God had given her, devoting him wholly to his service for life, holding the gift for God and not herself (the gift returned to God, 1 Chronicles 29:14) — the dedication (lent to the LORD as long as he liveth); 'And he worshipped the LORD there' (the worship that crowned the dedication, the family bowing before the LORD as the gift was given back, the consecration of the dearest to God, the worship of the consecrating soul, Romans 12:1; 1 Corinthians 6:19-20) — the figure (the soul that surrenders its dearest treasure to God, the providence, the dedication, Hannah's song of thanksgiving to follow, ch.2) — Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: What does Hannah's lending Samuel to the LORD signify?

sides.position: The believer who gives back to God the very gift received from him, devoting it wholly to his service - Hannah surrendering the son she begged entirely to the LORD's service for life, giving back to God what God had given, the child of prayer become the child of dedication, what we receive from God to be held as his and devoted to him (1 Chronicles 29:14), the believer's children and self wholly consecrated (Romans 12:1; 1 Corinthians 6:19-20)

themes: Therefore also I have lent him to the LORD; as long as he liveth he shall be lent to the LORD. And he worshipped the LORD there.
Hannah lending Samuel to the LORD as long as he lived (the crux of the believer who gives back to God the very gift received from him, devoting it wholly to his service)

the crown of Hannah's faith, that having received the son she begged she now surrenders him entirely to the LORD's service for life, giving back to God what God had given, the child of prayer become the child of dedication

what we receive from God to be held as his and devoted to him, the believer's children and self wholly consecrated to the LORD, the soul that surrenders its dearest treasure to God who gave us all (1 Chronicles 29:14; Romans 12:1; 1 Corinthians 6:19-20)
